summary: debugcli typosquat of `debug` ships postinstall credential/wallet stealer with Telegram exfiltration
details: npm package `debugcli` impersonates the popular `debug` package (index.js is `module.exports = require('debug')` with a runtime dependency on `debug@^4.3.4`) while its `postinstall` hook (`node install.js`) runs a credential and cryptocurrency-wallet stealer on installer machines. On install, install.js harvests installer-owned secrets from `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.ssh/config`, `~/.npmrc`, `~/.docker/config.json`, `~/.netrc`, `~/.pypirc`, git-credentials, Azure token files, and `.env` files walked up from the current working directory, and scrapes `process.env` for keys matching TOKEN/SECRET/APIKEY/AWS_/GITHUB_/NPM_/STRIPE/OPENAI patterns. It also enumerates Chrome extension Local Extension Settings directories for MetaMask (extension id nkbihfbeogaeaoehlefnkodbefgpgknn) and Trust Wallet (egjidjbpglichdcondbcbdnbeeppgdph), archives their vaults via PowerShell `Compress-Archive` on Windows or `tar czf` on Unix, and uploads both the collected secrets and the wallet archives via HTTPS to `api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage` and `/sendDocument`. The Telegram bot token and chat id are stored as XOR-encoded byte arrays; the XOR key is derived at runtime from a sha256 of the `lodash` package description fetched from `registry.npmjs.org`, an evasion technique to defeat static string scanning. Additional anti-analysis logic gates execution on sandbox heuristics (CPU/RAM, hostname regex for sandbox/cuckoo/anyrun/hybrid, SANDBOX/CUCKOO env, home-directory age), enforces a per-host 24h run lock using an MD5 of hostname+username in the temp directory, and self-deletes install.js after exfiltration completes.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
